data.title: SOXX: You Cost Too Much, But You Are Still Worth Even More
data.description:
Discussing positively in the comments with some of you readers, an observation you made really made me think, which paraphrased said something like: “I know little about semiconductors, I only know that these ETFs easily manage to outperform the average annual performance of the S&P 500.” And indeed the 10y CAGR of iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), my pick in semiconductors, is about +21%, with a 3y beta vs. S&P 500 (SP500) of 1.6 and a standard deviation of 31%.

Well, going beyond any other technical consideration, we certainly cannot say that that comment is wrong; it projects a disarming truth. And assuming to be “mentally immune” to volatility, it simply makes clear that today the only thing an analyst must be able to answer in my opinion is: is this CAGR sustainable? Well, personally, on SOXX I see a “two-paths” line of analysis: EPS and optimistic prospects, prohibitive valuations. And let me say that, in my opinion, the first will prevail over the second. But let’s go in order.

SOXX

Trying to make a recap of my first coverage, a new reader should simply remember that:

  • It remains among the most appreciated ETFs, not by chance it has an AUM (assets under management) of over $13 billion and a TER of 0.35%.

  • SOXX replicates the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX), which follows the performance of the 30 largest U.S.-listed companies in the semiconductor sector.
  • The concentration in the top 10 holdings is 58% while the sector distribution is characterized by: Semiconductors 79.5%, Semiconductor Equipment 20.4%.

  • Its distinctive feature compared to its competitor VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) is that SOXX is more exposed to fabless chip designers such as Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Broadcom (AVGO). SMH, on the other hand, has increased allocation to memory makers such as Micron (MU) and equipment suppliers such as ASML Holding (ASML), Applied Materials (AMAT), and Lam Research (LRCX). I invite you to look into this here.

If I had to describe its 2025 in one term it would be: V-shaped recovery. After the cut in expectations post liberation day, what in my opinion allowed it to recover, as discussed here, was an incentivizing geopolitical context, but above all the maintenance (despite tariffs) of EPS growth and competitive expectations within the same tech sector.

First line of thought: What worries analysts today?

Information technology with a forward P/E of 30x already has expected valuations above 93% of the 10-year distribution, and 84% of the 30-year one. If we look at CAPE, we are effectively at pre-dot.com bubble levels. Going more into detail on the semiconductor segment, SOXX has a P/E of 33.8x, so in relative terms it also presents valuations clearly above average, and statistically the higher the relative valuations, the more the expected return of the sector contracts.

Second line of thought: “growth expectations”

I start by making a statement I am convinced of, even if it is strong:

High P/Es, close to the level of the dot-com bubble, do not automatically mean the arrival of another bubble.

The difference between today’s tech companies and those of 2000 lies in profit margins. If on the one hand they share the P/E, in 2000 it was not justified, while today it is justified first of all by high profit margins in the tech sector.

And considering that specifically, the semiconductor segment, which in my opinion is well represented on the stock market by the NYSE Semiconductor Index (SOXX’s benchmark), makes up 40% of the tech sector, this explains:

  • Expected EPS growth: +45% YoY (for the semiconductor segment);
  • Expected revenue growth: +26% YoY.

So, how can one talk about a bubble in a sector with:

  • Upward guidance: many companies in the segment have provided positive outlooks, supported by AI, data centers, and demand for HBM (high-bandwidth memory);
  • While the percentage of semiconductor companies that beat expectations is above the historical average.

If the theory behind GARP investing (which I follow closely) is correct, a sector with these characteristics works with EPS compound growth: it generates enormous cash flows and reinvests them at high ROE. It can therefore still have competitive fwd EPS, an element that would sustain relative valuations at this level.

So, in my opinion, here the P/E gives a dimension of the market price, but absolutely not of its value.

Risk

In theory, it is a cyclical sector: it alternates boom and bust (historically) every 4 years, following somewhat the logic: capacity – overproduction – correction. So, every reasoning made on EPS does not eliminate the cyclical risk – the question is, will we reach a phase of overproduction?

Simply put, we cannot rule it out, and it is therefore a risk to live with, which in the end somewhat characterizes the negative side of that 21% CAGR return (a marked standard deviation and max drawdown). What, however, could mitigate this cycle? To answer, we should look at inventories. And it is reassuring in this sense that semiconductor companies are reducing stock faster than revenue growth. This, in my opinion, is a signal that demand is solid and not “bubble-driven.”

Conclusion

I am of the opinion that SOXX’s CAGR may indeed shrink due to its relatively expensive price. But I also think it will continue to be competitive compared to the S&P 500. I rule out the bubble scenario, the sector is expensive, but it also has value: we are talking about a sector with growth expectations in terms of revenues and earnings stronger than average, that beats expectations, and grows in guidance. On the negative side, its cyclical nature, with very high volatility.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: A Premium Moat, But Valuation Leaves Limited Upside
data.description:
Company Snapshot

The Procter & Gamble Company (NYSE: PG) is one of the world’s largest consumer staples companies, with iconic brands in fabric care (Tide, Ariel), in baby and family care (Pampers, Charmin), and in grooming (Gillette). It has a presence in more than 180 countries, and 5 billion people use a P&G product every day, making it a global powerhouse in the industry.

Shares are down 8.4% year-to-date (YTD) despite rising profitability, raising the question of whether further downside lies ahead. In this article, I explore whether PG represents value in the consumer staples sector or if investors should wait for a more attractive entry point.

Strong Moat & Future Outlook

PG’s greatest advantage lies in the strength of its global brand portfolio. With household names like Pampers, Tide, Gillette, and Olay, the company enjoys significant brand loyalty and strong pricing power across its business lines. We can see the evidence of this later in the fundamentals section. These brands allow PG to navigate inflationary environments and to pass on cost increases to consumers, a luxury that should not be underestimated. Similarly, the company’s scale in procurement, distribution, and marketing further strengthens its competitive position, giving it one of the strongest moats in the industry.

While growth in mature markets such as North America remains modest, PG has meaningful opportunities in emerging markets where there is an expanding middle class and growing disposable income. Markets such as India are expanding rapidly, and PG’s global distribution infrastructure could give it the edge here. Similarly, it is focusing on its e-commerce and digital channels. In its most recent earnings announcement, CFO Andre Schulten stated that e-commerce sales rose 12% and now accounted for 19% of total sales, a channel that, if sustained, offers long-term value.

It is a capital-light business compared to some of its peers. PG is consistently generating a strong free cash flow. This underpins one of the longest dividend growth streaks in the S&P 500 (69 consecutive years of increases), which is a major draw for income-focused investors. However, while returns are robust, growth rates are lower than those of its competitors. PG must balance maintaining shareholder returns organically and pursuing new lines or M&A opportunities that could enhance long-term growth.

Despite its advantages, PG faces meaningful headwinds. Intense competition from both global peers like Unilever (UL) and Colgate-Palmolive (CL) and local, low-cost brands pressures market share, particularly in emerging markets. Also, the current tariff situation is having an adverse impact on PG and its financials. In the Q4 2025 earnings call, Schulten highlighted that $1 billion of tariff-related costs are expected in FY26. If the tariff situation were to escalate, this could cause further deterioration of PG’s profitability.

Also, consumers are increasingly paying attention to a company’s sustainability and ethical initiatives. This puts pressure on companies such as PG to undertake ESG initiatives in order to maintain and grow its consumer base. PG has stated good ambitions in this regard with a commitment to reach net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2040 and by working to design all consumer packaging to be recyclable or reusable.

Moreover, while consumer staples businesses are resilient during economic downturns, premium products can suffer from a ‘trading down’ effect from consumers as they look to save costs. The current economic environment could drive existing consumers to cheaper alternatives or private labels, putting further pressure on PG to defend its market share.

Fundamentals: Standing Strong Against Competition

Let’s have a look at the financials of PG and where it sits in comparison to its peers.

Metric

Procter & Gamble

Colgate-Palmolive

Unilever

Net Profit Margin

18.95%

14.54%

9.29%

Gross Profit Margin

51.34%

60.62%

44.14%

Return On Capital Invested

15.60%

27.86%

12.65%

Debt/Capital Ratio

29.42%

72.82%

47.87%

P/E Ratio

23.54

22.19

22.54

Dividend Yield

2.69%

2.58%

3.35%

Book Value Per Share

$21.88

$0.87

$8.55

As the table shows, the company is comparable in its margins to its peers but has a better bottom line. Moreover, this net profit margin has been consistent over the last few years (it has been above 17% in every year since 2019). This consistency highlights PG’s cost discipline, which is a key advantage in a segment prone to margin pressures.

Moreover, in its most recent earnings announcement, PG stated earnings per share were up 6% versus the prior year. However, management did concede that while operating margin increased, core margin declined 70 basis points. This was largely attributed to input costs along with tariffs (Schulten highlighted that tariffs had a Q4 25 impact of $0.03 on its EPS).

While its return on capital is higher than that of Unilever’s, it is significantly below that of Colgate. This suggests that PG should be doing more to get the best return for its shareholders. However, the median rate for the sector is 7%, which makes PG a whopping 122% higher, and this suggests it’s performing admirably when compared to the industry as a whole. What’s more, it is higher than its 5-year average (14.9%), which suggests management is improving with regard to capital allocation.

The dividend yield compares well with that of its peers and has increased for 69 consecutive years. This is a key attraction for income-focused investors. Furthermore, its profitability and cash flow generating power suggest this growth can be sustained.

PG has a lower debt/capital ratio than its peers, which boosts both profitability and FCF, as less money is needed to service debt. This is valuable cash that can be invested elsewhere (such as in M&A pursuits, as suggested earlier in the thesis).

PG also has a strong overall balance sheet. It has a book value per share of $21.88 (which is significantly higher than that of its peers). This could explain its P/E ratio, which is higher than that of its peers. Given that it is not vastly higher than its peers, it could suggest that the premium placed on this company is lower than merited.

Valuation: What The Model Suggests

To aid the investment thesis, I ran a simplified discounted cash flow (DCF) model using the Gordon Growth Model. The model projects PG’s free cash flows (FCF) over the next five years, then applies a terminal growth rate to estimate long-term value. This method helps gauge what the business is worth today based on its future cash-generating ability.

Using reasonable assumptions set out below, the model yields a fair value estimate of approximately $115 per share, which is 25% below its current share price of $152 (as of 1 Oct.).

Assumptions used:

Metric

Input

Rationale

Discount Rate

7%

Risk-free rate + equity risk premium

Five-Year Growth Rate

4%

Just below 5-year average operating income increase

Terminal Rate

2%

Widely used rate in most models

FCF

$11.9bn

Normalised FCF (TTM) per most recent results.

The above suggests that PG is currently overvalued. However, it could mean that the market is simply willing to pay a premium for a company with a strong moat and a defensive nature. An important thing to note here is that it trades at a P/E ratio comparable to that of its peers, which suggests it could be fairly valued. Similarly, it generates stronger FCF and has a higher book value per share than its competitors, which reinforces this argument.

While no model is perfect, this exercise suggests that PG could be overvalued, as even with a growth rate of 6% (strong for a consumer staples business), it projects a lower price than the one at which it currently trades (assuming a discount rate of 7%).

Analyst Consensus

Wall Street analysts are bullish about PG. The stock currently holds a consensus rating of “Buy,” based on the latest assessments by analysts. Of 24 analysts, 10 rated the stock as “Strong Buy,” 4 as “Buy,” and 9 as “Hold.” Meanwhile, only one analyst recommended “Sell”.

Importantly, the average price target sits at $170.18, which implies a more than 10% upside from current levels ($152 at the time of writing).

Technical Insight

As of 2 October 2025, PG is trading at $152, down 8.4% YTD. It has dropped below its 200-day moving average and is currently trending downward. It has previously bounced off a $150 support (which was also an area of resistance in early 2024), and if it breaches this level, it could trigger further downside. It may be wise to wait for a confirmed turnaround before entering. Alternatively, for investors who trade with options, it could present a cash-secured-put selling opportunity.

What Would Change My Thesis?

Based on the above, I would classify PG as a Hold. What would make me change my thesis is largely how the current macro backdrop unfolds. If the tariff situation eases, that should boost PG’s margins, and profitability will increase. That will boost EPS and provide extra funds for reinvesting. Also, if the stock pulls back further below the $150 level (perhaps to a level that would classify it as oversold on the stochastic indicator) before signaling a reversal, it will present a good entry opportunity.

Conclusion

PG is a premium-quality defensive stock with exceptional dividend reliability, but the valuation looks full. It remains a solid hold for income investors, though new buyers may want to wait for more favorable entry conditions. This could be at a level below $150, or if tariffs ease and margins recover.

data.title: Main Street Capital: Rising Risks Mean Time To Run
data.description:
Ladies and gentlemen, chaos has reached the business development sector. Over the past month, the sector index as represented by the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), has declined by around 10%. The bad news bears have assembled as credit risks continue to increase, the Federal Reserve moves to lower the federal funds rate, and business development companies have nowhere to hide.

The past month has been tough on BDCs. Right now, the sector finds itself back in the doldrums of April when tariff madness was putting pressure on businesses around the world. Well, the tariff risk appears to have simmered when President Trump backed off the tariff gas pedal. Relative sanity returned to the sector and it began to climb once again. However, more recently, the seas have been stormy once again.

Some of the largest business development companies are running into trouble. For example, FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK) recently reported that the board of directors would be reassessing the dividend after its nonaccruals rose to 5.3% of the company’s portfolio.

Michael Craig Forman, CEO, reported net investment income of $0.62 per share and adjusted net investment income of $0.60 per share for the quarter, noting "our net asset value per share declined 6.2% from $23.37 to $21.93 during the quarter." He attributed these results to situations impacting four portfolio companies, which drove an increase in nonaccruals and a negative move in NAV.

As portfolio companies begin to feel the heat, investors have fled FSK, and over the past six months, the BDC has declined by around 30%.

Looking across the sector, performance has begun to diverge. More specifically, a small pocket of BDCs continues to trade at extraordinary valuations despite the industry beginning to show clear cracks. Today, we will revisit a top offender, Main Street Capital Corporation (NYSE:MAIN), which continues to trade at around double book value, despite an above-average nonaccrual risk.

Review of Prior Coverage

In July, I published an article on MAIN expressing caution around the company’s valuation. I focused on providing historical examples of valuation bubbles and the resulting trip back down to Earth. I even highlighted MAIN’s prior declines after reaching a similar valuation, such as the first three months of the year. Below is a summary:

At best, you will be buying into a compressed yield, hoping that the market continues to assess that MAIN deserves a 71.4% valuation premium to the sector average of 1.15x book value, according to Seeking Alpha. Each and every investor carries a unique perspective, risk tolerance, and thesis around how to make money.

While MAIN continued to trade at more than twice book value, I saw relatively little reason to be excited. I don’t see a possibility where the valuation continues to expand beyond twice book value, and the yield of the portfolio based on current share prices was nearly half of the company’s net asset value yield. It hardly seemed like a good deal!

Perhaps more worryingly, was the math behind a downside scenario for MAIN. Should the company return back to its median historical valuation, shares would fall by nearly 20%.

Over the past three months, shares of MAIN have been relatively stable, declining modestly. Shares are priced at $63 per share or 1.95x book value.

While three months is hardly enough time for an investment thesis to play out, shares of MAIN have begun to show weakness. Over the past month, shares of MAIN have begun a downward slide, declining by around 4% from earlier highs.

Right now, MAIN continues to trade at the sector premium by a mile according to BDC Investor.

Over the long term, I have explained why MAIN is likely to trade above book value and the corresponding benefit to their capital markets activity. However, this does not necessarily offer the same benefit for one buying those shares. With industry news becoming increasingly relevant, we should explore what could begin to spoil the party.

Times Are Changing

I see trouble coming towards some folks in the BDC sector. Specifically, those with the highest valuations and weakening fundamentals could see a reckoning if the market begins to change their opinion. One of my favorite quotes from Marks is about investor enthusiasm.

Whereas in real life things fluctuate between pretty good and not so hot, in the minds of investors, things can go from flawless to hopeless and back.

Right now, people look at MAIN as flawless, assigning a valuation to common shares that are twice the value of the underlying assets, according to their book value. This is uncommon for BDCs which typically trade closer to book value. I see several key issues coming towards MAIN and other business development companies.

First, rising non-accruals may have a significant impact on MAIN. Over the past year, there have been some emerging cracks in private lending such as rising payment-in-kind activity and rising non-accrual rates across investors.

The share of private credit borrowers that deferred at least some of their cash interest payments surged in the second quarter to the highest in almost four years, pointing to growing stress in the $1.7 trillion market, according to data from valuation firm Lincoln International. The percentage of debt investments with some sort of payment-in-kind increased to 11.4% in the quarter, according to Lincoln. That compares with 7.4% in the third quarter of 2021, when the firm began tracking the data.

Non-accruals continue to rise for MAIN, reaching a problematic level at the end of last quarter. MAIN ended the quarter with non-accruals comprising 2.1% of the portfolio by fair value and 5% by cost. Non-accruals on a fair value basis increased by 25% compared to 1.7% at the end of the previous quarter. Well, let’s compare the damage to FSK to assess the risk to MAIN.

Earlier, I mentioned that FSK has been forced to reassess their dividend as a result of rising non-accruals attributed to four portfolio companies. On a portfolio basis, non-accruals looked like this for FSK…

Nonaccruals increased to 5.3% of the portfolio on a cost basis and 3% on a fair value basis, compared to 3.5% and 2.1% respectively, in the previous quarter. The net asset value per share declined to $21.93.

FSK was forced to reassess their dividend going into 2026 when nonaccruals passed 5% of cost. As of last quarter, MAIN had also reached 5% of the portfolio on non-accrual. For me, it is difficult to believe that the company can earn a valuation premium of twice book value while 5% of originated loans are on non-accrual. Now, MAIN has several key differences that are worth noting. Foremost, the company’s equity investments provide a different business flavor that includes larger liquidity events and dividend payments from portfolio companies. This can increase flexibility in a way that insulates their business more than FSK.

More broadly, business development companies are at risk should the Federal Reserve begin to move benchmark rates more aggressively. While the recent cut of 25 basis points was largely symbolic and unlikely to have a significant impact economically, a larger cycle of cuts could be more impactful.

Previous rate cut cycles have negatively affected business development companies for various reasons. First and foremost, cash flow is reduced from floating-rate loans as SOFR moves alongside the federal funds rate.

While most BDCs have fixed rate financing and dividends, the reduction in cash flow is an immediate negative impact on the company. However, large rate cuts generally coincide with broader economic difficulties that increase risk. For example, rate cuts following the pandemic occurred when risk was significantly elevated and companies had already run into trouble. It can seem like the tail wagging the dog sometimes, as the causes and effects can overlap.

Outlook

With risks beginning to emerge, I see reason to be cautious around the BDC sector. More specifically, it’s important to look at the diverging pricing between key participants in the industry. While MAIN continues to defy gravity in its pricing, other competitors have been more impacted. For example, FSK, MAIN, and Ares Capital (ARCC) are some of the largest BDCs. Each has performed differently year to date.

Each also trades at a distinct price relative to book value.

Looking ahead, many of the issues facing these companies are systemic. All of them have rising non-accruals. All of them will be impacted by future changes to the federal funds rate. What will determine the future is management’s ability to navigate upcoming changes and the recognition of investors. MAIN earns a Strong Sell rating.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 239: $23,900 Allocated, $2,615.21 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
While the Fed started cutting again, the government headed toward a shutdown. The markets didn’t react well this week as the S&P 500 fell -0.65% while the Nasdaq retraced by -0.96%. I can’t believe that it’s almost October and Q3 earnings season is right around the corner. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio followed the market lower as its profitability declined by -1.91% (-%151.25). I’m expecting things to be choppy over the next several weeks because if an agreement isn’t reached and a government shutdown is forced it would lead to job furloughs and potentially used as permanent cuts. The last thing that I want to see is American’s lose jobs and unemployment rise even if it gets the Fed to cut rates at a quicker pace. Ultimately I think we will get an initial standoff but cooler heads will end up prevailing. I think that the political posturing in Washington will ultimately be a nothing burger for the market and earnings season will be much more important than what happens with the spending bill. October is going to be a big month for the market as the financials will lead us off in the middle of the month with earnings followed by the FOMC meeting at the end of the month.

We have now had 2 consecutive negative weeks which is healthy as I hate seeing stocks just go higher. After allocating $23,900 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 239 at $31,583.17. The account's profitability decreased by -1.91% (-$151.25) to $7,783.17, which is a return on invested capital of 32.70%. This week I added to my positions in ONEOK (OKE) and the Roundhill Innovation 100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $39.84 of dividend income in week 239. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $34.34 (1.33%) to $2,615.21. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,848.99 of income since its inception, which is 20.29% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio decreased in value this week, but I believe the account is positioned well for the future. It’s going to be interesting to see how the remainder of the year plays out, but I think that there could be close to $2,750 of forward dividend income being generated by the time 2025 comes to an end.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

This is the 2nd week that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has declined, which is healthy considering the run it's been on. When I look at the chart below, I am thrilled with the progress it's made. I started this portfolio to document how I would build an income-producing portfolio starting with a $100 and adding $100 per week going forward. My investment goals were to mitigate downside risk while generating recurring income. Capital appreciation was always a secondary goal. After 239 weeks, the portfolio is up $7,783.17 (32.70%) on invested capital and generating $2,615.21 in forward dividend income, which is a portfolio yield of 8.28%. I am excited to see how the next several years unfold, as there will be a tremendous amount of capital being reinvested back into the portfolio along with the weekly allocations.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $939.32 (35.92%)
  • Equities $589.76(22.55%)
  • CEFs $416.72 (15.93%)
  • REITs $416.27 (15.92%)
  • BDCs $253.15 (9.68%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 39 weeks in 2025, $1,669.31 of dividend income was produced, which is 102.84% of the total income from 2024. There were 567 positions that paid a dividend, which is 79.97% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $39.84 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $42.80. This is an increase of $11.59 (37.13%) YoY, and there have been 3 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I’m really excited for the rest of September to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

There are 2 days left in September, and after generating $39.84 of dividend income this week, it brought the September total to $138.67. I have a feeling there is going to be a lot of income generated in the last 2 days of the month. When I look at previous weeks in 2025, I exceeded $100 3 times. There is no reason why I can’t produce $60-$70 and get September above $200. I do think September will close with more than $200 generated, and last month could be the last time less than $200 is produced in a single month. I am excited to see how the chart below finishes the year, as I should be positioning myself to generate close to $2,700 of forward dividend income walking into 2026.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 44 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $460.64 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs have officially reached 20% of the portfolio again, and I added to REITs heading into the rate cut. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. Individual equities now represent 39.43% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.55% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.57% of the portfolio and generate 77.45% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

I know I have been saying this a lot, but I can’t believe that Altria Group (MO) continues to represent more than 4.5% of the portfolio. The amazing part is that I have not bought a single share since February 2024. Realty Income (O) and Enbridge (ENB) aren’t exactly working their way down toward the 3% level either. One of the keys to this portfolio’s success has been the level of diversification. Eventually, the top 3 positions will get smaller, and I will feel more comfortable allocating capital toward them. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

The top-10 positions haven’t since last week, but some dividends were generated. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,642.19, and they finished the week with a value of $9,262.31. This is an ROI of $2,620.79 (39.46%). There has been $1,229.31 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 18.51% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $646.67 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.74%. These positions represent 29.33% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 24.73% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 239 Additions

This week I added to ONEOK (OKE) and the Roundhill Innovation 100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE).

ONEOK

  • I just wrote an article on OKE (can be read here) that discussed my long-term investment thesis about this energy infrastructure giant.
  • I believe that ONEOK is strategically positioned to benefit from surging energy demand driven by AI, data center growth, and traditional power needs
  • With energy infrastructure critical to the AI revolution and Fed rate cuts making OKE's yield more attractive, now is a compelling entry point for long-term investors
  • Investors are able to secure a 5.65% dividend yield from OKE as a major data center buildout continues to accelerate, which will increase the need for traditional sources of energy

Roundhill Innovation 100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF

  • QDTE has been very interesting as it generates a weekly dividend and has an annualized yield of 37.03%
  • I needed another share or 2 so I could generate at least 1 share per year from its dividend
  • The ETF uses a synthetic covered call strategy on the Innovation-100 Index, selling 0DTE options to maximize income but limiting upside capture.
  • QDTE could continue to rebound off its lows while generating recurring weekly income

Week 240 gameplan

Next week is reader suggestion week so I will be adding a new position or adding to an existing position from the suggestions left in the comment section so please interact with the article.

Conclusion

September is almost over, and I have a feeling a lot of income is going to be generated in the last 2 trading days of the month, which should push the monthly income past the $200 level. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio continues to surprise me as the amount of forward dividend income being generated is exceeding my expectations. I am on track to generate $2,615.21 of annual dividend income which breaks down to $217.93 per month and Q4 hasn’t started yet. Anyone can build an income producing portfolio, all it takes is time and persistence. The S-curve is starting to form and I think 2026 is going to be an incredible year from a compounding perspective due to the amount of income being generated. Please leave all your thoughts in the comment section below as to which position I should add or add to next week. We’re heading into Q4 and I am excited to see how the remainder of the year unfolds.

data.title: Alphabet's Implied EPS Growth Expectations: Realistic Or Bubble?
data.description:
Alphabet Inc.'s (NASDAQ:GOOGL, NASDAQ:GOOG) trailing P/E ratio of approximately 26.50 implicitly reflects robust expectations for future earnings growth. What precisely are these expectations? Are these expectations likely to be realized?

To address these questions, our first step will be to "reverse engineer" the EPS growth expectations that are currently embedded in Alphabet's stock price. Our second step will be to assess whether and to what extent expectations of abnormal EPS growth are justified based on fundamentals. Our third step will be to perform a scenario analysis and to compare these scenarios to Alphabet's historical EPS performance and consensus EPS estimates. Finally, we will assess Alphabet's reward-to-risk profile.

Implied EPS Growth: The Analytical Framework

Our proprietary Abnormal Incremental Returns & Growth [AIRG] methodology provides an ideal framework for "reverse engineering" the real EPS growth rate expectation that is implicit in Alphabet's current share price. Our analysis requires three basic assumptions: 1) A Real Cost of Equity; 2) An abnormal growth forecast period that is explicitly defined; 3) Real earnings growth is expected to be "normal," in the continuing period beyond the explicit forecast horizon.

1. Real Cost of Equity (RCOE): 6.50%

In estimating the intrinsic P/E ratio of any stock, it is best to use the real (inflation-adjusted) cost of equity as the required real rate of return. We estimate Alphabet's Real (inflation-adjusted) Cost of Equity (RCOE) as the sum of three components:

  • Real risk-free rate: 2.50%. We use the real (inflation-adjusted) 30-Year TIPS yield as our benchmark for the real risk-free rate.
  • Equity market premium: 4.00%. The long-term historical equity premium (excess returns of equities relative to bonds) is approximately 5.00%. However, 4.00% is more consistent with estimates of the implied equity premium in recent decades and with independent estimates of the prospective equity premium.
  • Idiosyncratic premium/discount: 0.00%. This generous assumption is debatable. On the one hand, corporate bond yield spreads suggest that marginal investors are currently not pricing in a substantial risk premium for Alphabet’s long-erm debt. Fundamental measures of risk such as balance sheet strength, operating leverage and financial leverage are broadly supportive of a 0.00% idiosyncratic premium. However, both historical and implied volatility suggest that Alphabet investors are exposed to greater-than-average liquidity risk. Please note that although our base-case estimate of Alphabet's RCOE is 6.50%, our analysis below will consider the sensitivity of the estimate of price-implied real EPS growth to the estimated value of the RCOE.

2. Abnormal Growth Period: 10 Years

We have set our explicit abnormal earnings growth forecast period for Alphabet to 10 years, reflecting two considerations.

  • Proven moat: Alphabet has historically demonstrated a proven ability to sustain its competitive advantages (e.g., market share in Search) over time.
  • Maximum forecast period: We believe that 10 years represents the outer limit for a reasonable forecast of abnormal earnings growth in the rapidly evolving digital advertising and artificial intelligence industries.

3. Assumptions Beyond Year 10

  • Steady state. Beyond the 10th year, it is assumed that real EPS grows at a "normal" rate.
  • A normal real EPS growth rate is equal to the earnings retention rate multiplied by the real cost of equity.
  • Normal Terminal P/E. The intrinsic forward P/E ratio of a stock growing at a normal rate is the reciprocal of the stock's COE. In this case, the intrinsic P/E of a company with a real COE of 6.50% is 15.38. This is therefore the terminal forward P/E multiple for Alphabet applied at the end of year 10.

Alphabet's Implied EPS Growth: 11.48%

Based on the parameters above, the expected 10-year real EPS growth rate that is implicit in Alphabet's current stock price is 11.48% per annum. This assumes a continuation of its current retention rate of 91.46%.

The following is an analysis of the sensitivity of our estimate of the implied real EPS growth rate to the RCOE, plus a decomposition (normal vs. abnormal growth):

Sensitivity Table: Implied 10-Year Real EPS Growth Decomposition

Alphabet's price-implied expected 10-year real EPS growth rate of 11.48% (abnormal 5.54%) implies an expectation that the company will be able to sustain abnormally high returns (above COE) on incremental retained earnings for a full decade.

Context: History and Forecasts

In evaluating the reasonableness of Alphabet's implied EPS growth rate, it is instructive to compare it against its own historical performance as well as forward-looking analysts' forecasts.

  • Historical Growth: Between FY2015 and 2025E, Alphabet achieved a remarkable 10-year real EPS CAGR of 20.35%. The current price-implied 11.48% EPS growth expectation is significantly lower than this historical performance. This perhaps reflects recognition of the difficulty of sustaining such a pace of growth due to the "law of large numbers," and the maturation of the company/industry.
  • Median Forecast: The median sell-side analyst forecast for the next 5 years projects nominal EPS growth of approximately 13.5%, which translates to approximately 10.82% in real terms (based on 5-year TIPS break-evens). This is closely aligned with the price-implied real EPS growth rate of 11.48%.

In sum, the price-implied 10-year real EPS growth rate appears to be “reasonable.” It reflects a significant deceleration from historical growth rates and is consistent with the 5-year median forecast.

Analysis of Potential Drivers of Abnormal Incremental Returns and Abnormal Growth

Alphabet's stock price clearly reflects expectations of protracted abnormal incremental returns and abnormal EPS growth. Such performance can only be achieved if a company possesses an extraordinary moat built from sustainable competitive advantages. Is there a solid fundamental basis for these expectations?

Alphabet does indeed enjoy a unique set of competitive advantages, centered on its dominance of various digital spaces and its foundational capabilities in Artificial Intelligence.

  • Search Dominance: The core of Alphabet's moat is Google Search, which maintains a near-monopoly market share (>90% globally). This dominance is reinforced by massive data advantages (which improve search quality) and deeply habitual user behavior, making it the primary gateway to the internet.
  • Data Advantage and Ad Ecosystem: Alphabet possesses a comprehensive ad-tech stack across Search, YouTube, and the Google Display Network. This ecosystem provides unparalleled reach and targeting capabilities for advertisers. YouTube, in particular, benefits from massive engagement and the secular shift of advertising budgets toward Connected TV (CTV).
  • Google Cloud Platform: As the strong #3 global cloud infrastructure provider, GCP provides a significant growth vector. GCP specializes in data analytics and AI/ML services, leveraging the company's broader technological strengths.
  • AI Leadership (DeepMind/Gemini): Alphabet has long been a leader in foundational AI research and development. Its ability to integrate advanced AI (like the Gemini models) across its entire product suite is crucial for defending its existing moat and creating new revenue streams.

There is no question that Alphabet currently possesses a powerful moat. The real question is: For how long and to what extent can Alphabet continue to leverage its competitive advantages before competition (particularly AI-driven disruption) and/or regulation ultimately drive its profitability to normal levels?

The Bull Case: The AI-Reinforced Moat

In a bull-case scenario, Alphabet successfully navigates the transition to Generative AI, leveraging its technology to reinforce its dominance in Search and accelerate growth in the Cloud.

A 14% real EPS CAGR over a 10-year period is potentially achievable in this bull-case scenario. This growth rate is above the consensus 5-year forecast (10.82% real) but still well below Alphabet's historical growth (20.35% real).

Bull-Case Drivers:

  • AI Integration Revitalizes Search: In this scenario, the integration of advanced AI (Gemini) and the evolution of the Search Generative Experience (SGE) enhance the utility of Google Search. This wards off disruption from alternative AI interfaces (like ChatGPT) and potentially increases monetization efficiency.
  • GCP Acceleration and Margin Expansion: GCP growth accelerates, driven by demand for Alphabet's differentiated AI/ML offerings. Crucially, in this scenario, GCP achieves significant operating leverage, and its margins begin to converge toward the levels of Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS and Microsoft’s (MSFT) Azure.
  • YouTube Dominates Connected TV: YouTube is able to successfully capture the lion's share of the advertising budgets shifting from linear television to Connected TV. This drives strong revenue growth and enhances the overall advertising ecosystem.
  • "Other Bets" Discipline and Optionality: Management maintains capital discipline regarding "Other Bets," reducing the drag on consolidated ROIC. Potential monetization or spin-off of specific ventures (e.g., Waymo) could unlock shareholder value.

Bull Case Valuation:

If we assume Alphabet achieves 14% real annual EPS growth for the next 10 years, the intrinsic forward P/E ratio derived from our model is 29.35x. Compared to the current forward P/E of 24.09x, this implies a bull case upside of +21.8%.

The Moderate Bear Case: Disruption and Regulation

Given the valuation of its shares, Alphabet's investors could earn disappointing returns in a positive fundamental scenario that is simply "strong," rather than "exceptional." A moderate "bear-case" scenario would involve a combination of technological disruption eroding the Search moat and regulatory actions impairing its business model.

A robust 8% real EPS CAGR over 10 years (which is still abnormal) could be achievable in such a moderately strong scenario. This growth rate is below the current 5-year consensus forecast (10.82% real) and the price-implied expected EPS growth rate of 11.48%.

Bear Case Drivers:

  • AI Disrupts Search Dominance: In this scenario, Generative AI fundamentally changes user behavior. Users increasingly rely on direct answer engines and AI agents, reducing the volume of high-intent commercial searches on Google and impairing monetization.
  • Regulatory Headwinds and Remedies: The ongoing DOJ lawsuit in the U.S. and regulatory actions in the EU could result in significant remedies. These could include forced structural changes to the ad-tech business or alterations to default search agreements, impairing profitability.
  • Cloud Competition Intensifies: GCP remains a distant third to Azure and AWS. Intense competition prevents GCP from achieving the scale necessary for significant margin expansion, and it continues to require heavy capital investment.
  • Rising Traffic Acquisition Costs (TAC): The costs required to maintain default search status on mobile platforms (e.g., payments to Apple) rise faster than revenue. This compresses margins in the core Search business.
  • Depreciation policy. Google management is depreciating its AI infrastructure assets at a slower rate than Amazon or Microsoft. This provides a major relative boost to current and projected EPS. Given the pace of technological change, obsolescence of AI infrastructure investments could occur sooner than management expects. This would bring about higher depreciation rates and/or charge-offs in the future, depressing future EPS growth.

Bear Case Valuation:

If Alphabet were estimated to generate a still-strong 8% real EPS CAGR for 10 years, the intrinsic forward P/E drops to 18.21x from the current forward P/E of 24.09x. This implies a bear case downside of -24.4%.

Overall Assessment of Reward-to-Risk Profile

Our scenario analysis above does not represent an independent assessment of different potential outcomes. Rather, it represents a projection of the sorts of scenarios that would be required to achieve the posited real EPS growth rates. This scenario analysis presents a relatively balanced, slightly unfavorable risk/reward profile: +21.8% upside versus -24.4% downside—a reward-to-risk ratio of 0.89 to 1.00.

In assessing the probabilities of all possible outcomes, we think that they are skewed to the downside. The implied growth (11.48%) aligns closely with consensus (10.82%). However, when we consider the entire range of possibilities, we think that there is a higher probability of an outcome that falls short of the price-implied real EPS growth expectation, than of an outcome that exceeds it. In particular, the existential risks posed by AI disruption, combined with significant regulatory overhangs, create substantial uncertainty.

Therefore, we do not think Alphabet's stock is particularly attractive on an "expected return" basis.

Furthermore, based on our own application of the "margin of safety" principle, we seek a reward-to-risk ratio of at least 2:1 and an upside-downside absolute differential of at least 25%. Alphabet's stock fails on both of these criteria.

Having said that, we do not think that the demand structure underlying Alphabet's share price is inherently unstable (i.e., bubble susceptible to a crash). As long as Alphabet's current implied real EPS growth rate is merely plausible, its current valuation is likely to continue to be supported by an "optimistic fringe" of market participants.

Conclusion

An investment in Alphabet at today's prices is ultimately a bet that the company can achieve abnormal returns on incremental investments, which will, in turn, enable it to deliver abnormally high real EPS growth of 11.48% for the next 10 years.

Although we are quite bullish on Google’s fundamentals, we think that Alphabet’s current price-implied expected real EPS growth rate of 11.48% is somewhat of a stretch. However, we do think that it is at least plausible. For this reason, we think that the current valuation can continue to be supported by an "optimistic fringe" of market participants.

All factors considered, we rate Alphabet as a "Neutral" or "Market-Weight" for managers that are index-sensitive. For investors that seek superior risk-adjusted returns, as well as attractive absolute long-term returns, we do not think that Alphabet's shares offer a sufficiently compelling value proposition.

At Successful Portfolio Strategy, we continue to concentrate on finding opportunities that offer better reward-to-risk profiles, particularly outside the United States.

data.title: 3 Way-Too-Early Predictions For 2026
data.description:
I thought I would do something different for my column today. I will try to peer out into the next year and make three way-too-early predictions for 2026. They follow below.

Inflation Hits Fed's Target Rate:

Inflation has been 'sticky' and above the Federal Reserve's two percent target throughout 2025. This has complicated the central bank's ability to lower rates even as the jobs markets have clearly started to deteriorate over the summer.

The good news is inflation will likely fall under the Fed's official target in 2026 and pave the way for further reductions in the Fed Funds rate. I say this for two key reasons. First, the shelter component, which makes up just over 30% of the CPI calculation, consists of Owners' equivalent rent of residences or OER and rent of primary residence or RENT.

Both of these components will benefit from falling rents and declining home prices in 2026. Falling birth rates combined with mass re-migration are having a notable impact on population growth. In fact, 2025 might go down as the first year in U.S. history where the nation's population declined from the previous year.

These factors are already impacting the rental market. It should be noted that median rents in August fell 0.9% from the same month a year ago. The rental vacancy rate was also at 7.1%. This is the highest vacancy rate since Apartment List started this survey in 2017. In a report that came out this week, rents declined again in September. This time by .4% on a year-over-year basis.

On the housing side of the equation, the average price of a new home was under that of an existing home in both June and July. This is the first time this has happened since records started to be kept. In addition, there are more home sellers than home buyers than at any point in over a decade. Given this, it is hard to see how average home prices do not decline in the year ahead.

The second reason I think inflation moves down to the Fed's target rate in 2026 is covered in the section below.

U.S. Enters A Recession:

I am not expecting a major recession in 2026, but I do see the U.S. economy entering into at least a technical definition (two quarters in a row of negative GDP) next year. I think this scenario is at least a coin flip despite impressive GDP growth in Q2 and predicted solid GDP growth in Q3 currently.

Jobs growth is clearly faltering. The August BLS report showed only 22,000 jobs being created during the month. This was far under the expectations of 75,000. In addition, the original June and July BLS jobs numbers were also revised down sharply. Then, on Wednesday, the September ADP jobs report showed the country lost 32,000 jobs in September. The September BLS report will not come out until after the current government shutdown ends.

The economy is almost entirely dependent on the surge of tech spending connected to the AI revolution and massive fiscal deficit spending for growth. The latter of which is unsustainable and the former is likely to slow in coming years due to funding and other constraints. The military industrial complex is also doing well, with the defense budget getting a 13% boost for FY2026.

The rest of the market is struggling. I covered the challenged housing and rental sectors in the first section. The commercial real estate sector is seeing fast-rising delinquency rates and will have to refinance a good chunk of the just less than $5 trillion in CRE debt outstanding at higher rates in the coming years.

The consumer, which drives nearly 70% of U.S. economic activity, is under increasing pressure. I covered this in a recent article, and consumer sentiment continues to be dismal. Finally, the Leading Economic Indicators are also pointing to a recession ahead.

Healthcare Outperforms:

My final prediction for 2026 is that the healthcare sector will outperform the overall market. This is based on several observations. First, Healthcare is a defensive sector. It almost always outperforms the overall market historically when the economy is slowing quickly or enters a recession, which seems a likely scenario in the year ahead.

It also should be mentioned that the head of pharmaceutical research at BMO Capital Markets highlighted on CNBC this week that valuations for the pharma sector were approaching a 15-year low. There are also lots of names in this space like Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), Pfizer Inc. (PFE), Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY), and Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), all of which trade under 15 times forward earnings and provide more than solid dividend yields.

And that is my early list of market predictions for 2026. If you have a market prediction for 2026, please feel free to post it in the comments below.

data.title: BYD: Irrationally Underpriced
data.description:
Chinese EV maker BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF) reported decent delivery results for the month of September this week, with robust growth in its core battery-powered passenger EV business. BYD last year overtook Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) as the largest EV volume producer and has a significantly higher delivery volume than other Chinese electric vehicle companies as well. However, a fierce price and market share war in China has led to a steep profit drop in the second quarter which created selling pressure for the firm's shares. BYD is extremely attractively priced currently and has very low risk, in my opinion. I like BYD’s massive delivery volume, strong gross profit picture as well as low valuation based on forward revenue.

Previous rating

I rated shares of BYD a strong buy in my last article on the EV manufacturer -- A Better Bet Than Tesla -- in part because the company was a leader in terms of gross margins in the electric vehicle industry group and the EV company was selling an impressive amount of electric vehicles, both at home and internationally. In September, BYD saw solid delivery gains in the BEV segment. I believe shares of BYD are an attractive investment for investors who want to invest in a company that offers both profitability and strong growth prospects.

Strong BEV segment performance in September 2025

BYD experiences some solid growth in its BEV passenger segment: in September 2025, the Chinese EV giant sold 205,050 battery-powered EVs, showing 24.3% year-on-year. On a month-over-month basis, deliveries in the important BEV segment increased 2.7%. The firm's total new energy vehicles—including battery-powered EVs as well as plug-in hybrids—came in at 393,060 units, showing a slight drop-off of 5.9% year-over-year.

According to CarNewsChina, BYD has by far the biggest monthly sales volume in the Chinese electric vehicle market. In September, the EV company sold 393k passenger new energy vehicles, including both BEVs and plug-in hybrids, which firmly establishes BYD as the top company in China: its passenger EV sales in September were equal to ~2.4X the sales volume of the second-biggest company, Geely Auto.

BYD overtook Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) last year as the world’s largest EV producer. Tesla massively rotated out of favor with investors in the first half of the year, in large part because Tesla suffered steep delivery declines in Q1 and Q2. The firm's Q3 results, however, showed strong growth (+7.4% Y/Y) as buyers piled into EVs before tax credits expired on September 30, 2025. In the third quarter, Tesla delivered a massive 497,099 EVs, a quarterly record, easily beating the consensus estimate of 447,600 electric vehicles. I discussed the latest delivery results of Chinese EV start-ups in my work on NIO: Strong Onvo-Led Growth Upside.

A key driver of BYD's sales and delivery growth is its booming exports, especially in markets like Latin America, Southeast Asia, and Europe. In September, the EV enterprise maintained considerable growth momentum here and exported a total of 71,256 NEVs overseas, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 116%, with products like the BYD ATTO or the Seagull being especially instrumental in driving the firm's growth.

In the second quarter, BYD's net profit plunged 30% year-over-year to 6.4B Chinese Yuan ($892M) which resulted in selling pressure for BYD's shares… which pretty much lasts to this day.

I believe, however, that the correction constitutes a buying opportunity, especially for investors that want to own one of the largest and most profitable EV companies in the world today. As the company scales its operating footprint and focuses on markets outside of China, which is where competition is less aggressive than in its home market, BYD can create a catalyst for a share revaluation to the upside.

From a total gross profit perspective, BYD is still the most profitable EV company in the world, although growing pricing pressure in the domestic market has recently weighed on the firm's profitability: in the second quarter, BYD's gross margin fell to 14.7% while Tesla, the biggest direct competitor, generated a margin of 17.2%. In fact, Tesla's margin resilience was one reason why I recommended shares of the EV company earlier this year (I have since changed my rating to sell). Li Auto Inc. (LI) has the highest margins in its core manufacturing business, in part because the EV enterprise follows a strategy of serving the market for premium EV buyers.

BYD: Massive valuation advantage compared to Tesla

BYD has massive scale and is already profitable which gives the company a serious competitive advantage in a market that is seeing escalating price competition. Shares of BYD are currently valued at a forward price-to-revenue ratio of 0.86X and the EV maker is expected to generate ~18% top-line growth next year.

For comparison, shares of Tesla, which reported steep declines in deliveries in its core markets in the first half of the year—the U.S., Europe, and China—are trading at a forward P/S ratio of 13.8X which makes the EV company by far the most expensive electric vehicle play for investors. Due to this stretched valuation, I recently took profits: Tesla: Why I Am Cashing Out Now (Rating Downgrade).

The main reason for this massive valuation discrepancy between BYD and Tesla is that U.S. investors are hesitant to invest in Chinese EV companies due to various reasons, including transparency and governance concerns as well as past government crackdowns on the tech sector. However, BYD is the biggest EV company in China and should be able to continue to grow its top line as it expands its operations inside and outside of China.

In my opinion, BYD could easily revalue to a ~2.0X P/S ratio, under the condition that the EV maker reverses its earnings trend and maintains its growth momentum, especially in its export markets as well as in China. A ~2.0X P/S ratio would translate to a fair value for the EV maker's shares in the neighborhood of $33.50/share and imply more than 133% upside revaluation potential.

Company Revenue FY 2026 ($B) Y/Y Growth Forward P/S
BYD $149.76 17.5% 0.86X
Tesla $110.80 19.1% 13.80X
Li Auto $25.70 40.0% 1.00X
NIO $17.80 42.1% 1.06X
XPeng $16.26 44.4% 1.39X
Average: 3.62X
Average ex-TSLA: 1.08X

Source: Author

Risks with BYD

BYD is focused on establishing itself as the worldwide leader in low-cost EV production which obviously comes with certain risks, especially as margins are concerned. However, BYD is the largest volume producer in the EV industry in China which means the EV enterprise is uniquely suited to endure an extended price war in the domestic market. What would change my mind about BYD is if the EV firm were to see growing pressure on its earnings (gross profit margins) or if the company were to scale back its international expansion plans.

Final thoughts

BYD reported decent results in its BEV segment for the month of September and the EV enterprise remains a serious player in the global EV market. It still clearly dominates in China. Tesla reported a very impressive third-quarter delivery report card as well which is mainly due to buyers jumping in before federal tax subsidies were set to expire at the end of September.

What I like most about BYD is the company’s solid core business profitability (despite pricing and earnings pressure) paired with an extremely low valuation based on revenue: shares of BYD are trading for just 0.86X FY 2026 revenue, and BYD is actually one of only a small number of EV companies that are already profitable. Therefore, I see a very favorable risk profile and believe the EV firm has a ton of revaluation upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fair Isaac: A Pricing Shift Wall Street Didn't See Coming
data.description:
Fair Isaac Corporation (NYSE:FICO), the credit-scoring firm commonly referred to as FICO, gained 18% on October 2 after announcing a big change to its licensing model that cuts out the middleman (credit bureau companies) and can potentially boost its profitability.

It has been almost one year since I wrote about FICO. Last year, I argued that FICO's valuation was getting ridiculous -- despite the company having a competitive advantage -- as the stock's price growth was outpacing EPS growth. Since then, FICO stock has fallen 12%, while the S&P 500 Index (SPX) has gained 14%, changing the picture a little bit.

Today, the company is still dominant, and the stock is much closer to a fair valuation. However, it's still not an obvious buy, in my view, and I'd prefer a lower price. Thus, I reiterate a Hold rating.

FICO's Surprise License Model Shift

One of the most important pieces of news since my last FICO article came just recently on October 1, when FICO announced a change in the way it is delivering its FICO® Scores for mortgages. The company announced a "new direct-to-reseller license approach," cutting out the middleman and cutting the cost by up to 50% for per-score FICO fees. Lenders will now have two options: the new performance model, or the traditional pricing model.

Regarding the new performance model, the press release stated:

Under the new performance model, the royalty fee for the FICO Score will be $4.95 per score, which represents a 50% reduction in average per score fees into the tri-merge resellers, a reduction achieved by eliminating credit bureau mark-ups. A funded loan fee of $33 per borrower per score will apply when a FICO-scored loan is closed, recognizing the FICO Score’s downstream utility for mortgage insurers, GSEs, investors, rating agencies, and other market participants.

Regarding the traditional pricing model, here's what the press release stated:

Alternatively, lenders may opt to continue using the current per score only pricing model, which maintains a $10 per score fee into the tri-merge resellers, the average price previously charged by credit bureaus for the FICO® Score. This model is designed to represent no increase in per score fees for lenders.

This is a major change because historically, FICO has distributed scores to mortgage lenders through the three major credit bureaus -- Equifax (EFX), TransUnion (TRU), and Experian (OTCQX:EXPGY) -- who then marked up the prices. The new model allows tri-merge resellers (companies that combine credit reports from multiple bureaus) to calculate and deliver scores directly to customers. This caused shares of the aforementioned credit bureau companies to fall. Equifax stock fell 8.5%, while TransUnion and Experian fell 10.64% and 4.3%, respectively.

Why Did This Send FICO Stock Higher?

Investors liked the move because it can improve FICO's margins. Instead of collecting a percentage of the old $10-per-score fee, FICO will capture $4.95 plus a $33 funded-loan fee if a mortgage closes. This could help lift margins as mortgage volumes recover. During the Q3 earnings call, FICO's CEO, William Lansing, even expressed optimism about mortgage volume growth going forward, especially as rates decline. So that's another positive to think about.

Also, the new pricing model reduces FICO's dependence on the three big credit bureaus, who also own VantageScore, a rival to FICO. By cutting out the credit bureaus, FICO removes the incentive for the bureaus to steer lenders toward VantageScore.

Analysts at Barclays (BCS) said that the move is a "clear positive" for Fair Isaac Corporation, stating that it will raise mortgage-related revenue per score from about $4.95 to $10.

That sounds a bit confusing since the press release states that the price will be lowered to $4.95, so why did Barclays say it will raise the revenue per score to $10? My understanding is that the company possibly got around $4.95 under its old model, while the other ~$5 went to a credit bureau. Now, FICO can get $4.95 cleanly and sometimes get a $33 funded-loan fee when a mortgage actually closes, possibly bringing the average mortgage-related revenue per score closer to $10.

In any case, Barclays raised its EPS estimates "by 20–25% across all out-years" and raised its FICO stock price target from $2,000 to $2,400.

Barclays also stated that credit bureaus collectively generate over $200 million per year from reselling FICO scores at margins greater than 40%, so it makes sense that their shares sold off.

FICO's Business Has Remained Strong

Even if you put the recent news aside, FICO's business has remained strong since I last wrote about it. In its fiscal third quarter (for the period ended June 30, 2025), the company's revenue grew by 20% year-over-year to $536 million, and non-GAAP EPS reached $8.57 compared to $6.25 the year earlier. Free cash flow also hit $276.2 million in the quarter compared to $205.7. TTM free cash flow per share is now $31.84 and growing steadily.

The company's gross profit margin keeps expanding as well, with the TTM gross profit margin at 81.7% compared to 79.7% in Fiscal 2024.

In fact, its major profitability metrics are all relatively high compared to the sector median and are higher than the company's five-year average. FICO stock actually has a profitability grade of A+, and I imagine the profitability will only improve from here with the recent news.

Fair Isaac Stock Valuation: Not Ridiculous Anymore

When I covered FICO last October, the stock's GAAP P/E ratio was 108x, which was already a reason to be cautious. I even argued that the high valuation would make buybacks less effective and slow down EPS growth a bit. Now, the stock's GAAP P/E ratio is 70x, which is more palatable for sure, especially with the prospect of improving profitability due to the pricing shift.

Prior to the recent news release, EPS estimates for Fiscal 2026 were $35.57. If these estimates get revised, say, 20% higher (similar to Barclays' revision), that would give you an estimated $42.68 in EPS for the next fiscal year. Based on a share price of ~$1,785, that's a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 41.8x.

That's actually similar to its five-year average forward P/E ratio (one year out) of 42.9x.

Still, there are two caveats. First, we don't know whether all analysts will raise estimates by ~20% yet (or whether FICO's EPS will actually see that much of a boost). Second, yes, the stock would be near the average valuation, but that doesn't always mean it's an amazing deal. What if the average in the past five years was just too high to begin with?

Nonetheless, I think it's fair to say that the stock's valuation is much better now than it was last year, especially with FICO's ability to consistently grow EPS at above 20% annually. It's possibly fairly priced, but I'd prefer a drop to give me a better margin of safety.

A gap fill in the chart would happen at around $1,535, which would be 14% lower than the current share price. That would start to get me interested.

The Takeaway

FICO's new licensing model is great news for the company, so it makes sense that the stock jumped 18% following the announcement. By cutting out the middleman and adding a funded-loan fee, Fair Isaac is positioned to increase its profitability while also reducing the risk of credit bureaus pushing VantageScore as an alternative.

Combined with another strong quarter for the company and continually improving profitability metrics, FICO's business remains incredibly strong.

That said, even with a more "normal" valuation now, there's still not a compelling margin of safety for me. Investors that aren't interested in market timing and just want a great business at a valuation that's not ridiculous anymore can consider FICO stock, but I personally want the stock to come down a bit further (at least to the gap fill area for now) before considering it. Therefore, I reiterate my Hold rating.

data.title: NXP Semiconductors: Cyclical Chip Maker At Fair Price
data.description:
NXP Semiconductors (NASDAQ:NXPI) is a fairly large semiconductor company, with a market cap around $56B. While smaller chip makers usually lack much upside, providing niche products, I believe NXP shows the cyclical signs for which these smaller players are known and trades at a fair price for that.

Financial History

The previous decade of financial results shows a trend of long-term growth, marked by cyclical pressures.

For example, between 2015 and 2024, annual revenue more than doubled. There are rises and falls, but the falls tend to be much flatter than the rises.

While some companies might have some cash burn on the down cycle, NXP generally generates cash. Free cash flow was consistently positive over the past decade. I've included an adjusted FCF figure as well, to reflect the impacts of M&A, divestitures, and stock-based compensation. The earlier half of the decade featured more acquisitions and divestitures, hence why FCF and AFCF diverged more.

Looking at how cash is returned to shareholders, buybacks have occurred every year, and dividends have occurred since 2018. Buybacks are typically well in excess of dividends.

Buybacks have always been in excess of stock-based compensation, which is why I prepared an AFCF figure, to show the effects of how SBC is realized as an effective cash expense.

The current year-to-date results show lower sales and gross profits, but it is marginal at worst.

Operating cash flows also showed a slight decline for the year. More impactful was a $679M acquisition.

Q2 ended with about $3.1B in cash against more than $10B in debt. It's not a struggling balance sheet, but it's also not something I would consider a fortress, especially with known cyclical factors.

The maturities are significantly staggered out over more than a decade and bear fairly low-interest rates, indicating the credit quality and cost of capital are at least good. Despite the large balance, it's a manageable debt profile.

Activity continued into Q3. $950M was spent on another acquisition, and another bond offering of $1.5B was issued.

Overall, it's a financial history that looks like treading water and maintaining balance.

Business and Revenue Breakdown

NXP might be best described as a producer of chips that serves a variety of consumer products.

Their portfolio of semiconductors serves the following end markets:

  • Automotive
  • Industrial & IoT
  • Mobile
  • Comm Infra & Other

Recent years indicate that a majority of revenue is attributed to the automotive end market, so NXP is most exposed to trends in this consumer market over time. Activity in the other markets is still substantial, taken as a whole.

Outlook and Valuation

Overall, I consider NXPI to be fairly valued. It's not a terribly risky business, but among the bond issues, the M&A, the buybacks/dividends, and stock-based compensation, all in a cyclical business, it's spinning a lot of plates. This makes it seem hard for significant value to be unlocked on an up cycle.

CEO Kurt Siever spoke to his view on upside in the Q2 earnings call, this July:

So in summary, NXP's second quarter results and guidance for the third quarter reflect an increasingly positive view that a new up cycle is beginning to materialize. This is based on several signals we track regularly. These include continually growing customer backlog levels placed with our distribution partners, improved order signals from our direct customers, increased short-cycle orders and increasing product shortages leading to customer escalations.

In their annual report, management also notes that demand for their products has grown over time, as more cars become "smart" and as handheld devices become increasingly complex. The average number of chips that end users (automotive, industrial, IoT, mobile, and comms) all need increases.

The evolution of vehicles into self-driving, as well as the recent booms of AI and autonomous technology beyond automobiles—these all represent important use cases and cause for near-term growth. While I mentioned that revenue roughly doubled from 2015 to 2024, I did not mention that operating expenses also doubled, from about $1.5B to $3B in that same period. FCF growth is therefore largely a game of maintaining gross margins and increasing volume, which is basically what NXP did in that decade.

Recent years (with the flatter revenue) showed that FCF/AFCF levels are $1.5B on the low end and $2.8B on the high end. A period of growth could conceivably see cash flows reach into a range of $3B to 4B. That would suggest the market cap is a current multiple of about 14 to 18, which I think is a growth multiple for them.

Over time, they have managed to increase gross margins steadily. As they scale with M&A, their ability to manage the cost of materials can improve, but increasing margins on the pricing level is tough. As their products have primarily consumer end uses, the final product cannot be priced so high that consumers will not buy them. The chips of Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), and Astera Labs (ALAB), by comparison, have more upside in their pricing because their primary use has been productivity, not consumption.

For that reason, we can reasonably imagine that NXP will steadily grow over time, and even the low-end multiple of 14 is what I consider fair for steady growth. I believe it would be more practical to take a position with a market cap under $35B, should such a valuation present itself. This would follow a share price below $160.

As Q3 results approach, we will want to see if there is a basis for more upside. Similarly, we will want to see if there have since been realized impacts from the tariffs, which management had mentioned were not impactful during the first two quarters.

Conclusion

NXP has a diverse portfolio of chips to adaptively serve different use cases and consumer trends. Its model is not capital-light, however, and it's a regular balancing act in its capital allocation. As the market cap represents a growth multiple for what is a cyclical that will have only steady growth over time at best, I consider it fairly valued and initiate coverage with a Hold.

data.title: XOMA Royalty PFD Update: Going Back To My Original Sell Ratings
data.description:
Introduction

XOMA Royalty Corporation (NASDAQ:XOMA) offers preferred stock investors the opportunity to diversify their holdings away from mREITs and banks, the main issuers of these securities, without having to own ETFs or CEFs to accomplish that task. Along with a brief overview of XOMA Corporation, I will review both preferred stocks available:

  • XOMA Corporation 8.375% DP PFD B (NASDAQ:XOMAO)
  • XOMA Corporation 8.625% CUM PERP PFD SER A (NASDAQ:XOMAP)

Based on XOMA's last financing deal at a higher interest rate, and low asset coverage, while I do not see either issue being a Call risk currently, both are now rated as a Sell. There are strong regional banks offering the same yield.

XOMA Royalty Corporation review

Seeking Alpha provides this description of the company:

XOMA Corporation operates as a biotech royalty aggregator in Europe, the United States, and the Asia Pacific. The company engages in helping biotech companies for enhancing human health. It acquires the potential future economics associated with pre-commercial therapeutic candidates that have been licensed to pharmaceutical or biotechnology companies. The company focuses on early to mid-stage clinical assets primarily in Phase 1 and 2 with commercial sales potential that are licensed to partners. The company was incorporated in 1981.

XOMA describes its strategy as:

XOMA plays a unique role in helping biotech companies achieve their goal of improving human health. We do this by acquiring the economic rights to future milestone and royalty payments associated with partnered pre-commercial clinical candidates. In return the seller receives non-dilutive, non-recourse funding to advance their internal drug candidate(s).

Source: XOMA - A biotech royalty aggregator.

From the recent Investors Relations presentation, the first chart shows the strategy XOMA is executing.

They highlighted the following assets in their portfolio.

The August 2025 presentation listed several pending events they highlighted.

Now looking at the financials, the Income Statement shows XOMA has a problem of staying profitable.

When viewing the Balance Sheet, common shares outstanding has hovered around 12MM, not the diluted value shown above (17MM). Important data from the Balance Sheet is shown next.

Critical points include:

  • The difference between the Book Value and Tangible Book Value [TBV], which is much less. The TBV definition says it is a better measure of the real assets held by XOMA.
  • The $20MM difference between the 6/25 Total Common Equity and Total Equity was from convertible preferred stock issued to BVF in 2017 and since converted into common shares. XOMA statements do consistently list this value.
  • Statements from XOMA confirm the Total Common Equity [TCE] value of $72MM.

This recent Seeking Alpha article is recommended reading as part of any investor's due diligence process. It covers in depth the strategy change XOMA Royalty has implemented.

Preferred stock review

Factor XOMAO XOMAP
Issued 4/5/21 12/11/20
Size (MM) $40 $24.6
Coupon 8.375% 8.625%
Series letter B A
Price $25.62 $26.58
Yield 8.2% 8.1%
Call date 4/15/22 12/15/25
YTC NA NEG

Neither issue was rated at issuance. Both are eligible for the reduced tax rate and dividends are cumulative, a feature missing from most bank-issued preferred stocks.

Analysis

  • Both issues were oversubscribed.
  • Both are trading above Par thus the possibility of incurring a negative return if Called. That said, the market appears to be discounting that happening soon.
  • Trading volume is almost non-existent, making opening or closing a large position difficult or having to accept a price hit.
  • In a few months, both issues will be past their Call date. Based on coupon, XOMAP will be Called (partial or full) first.
  • The conversion of its preferred shares by BVF account for 27% of the Common Equity coverage dollars.
  • Quarterly earnings are highly variable though the strategy is the recent acquisitions will smooth EPS out: too early to tell.
  • Annual cash flow has been negative since 2021.
  • Current cash held is 3X what is needed to Call XOMAP.

Conclusion

Listed in the Investor Relations report is that XOMA received $130MM in financing from Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) at a cost of 9.875%, secured only by VABYSMO royalties. XOMA can draw another $10MM. Remember, debt is higher in the capital structure even with the limited backing. Add the fact that the TCE is less than the value of the three preferred stocks. I would rate both issues as high risk, thus I am restating my Sell rating on both preferreds: similar yields from stronger issuers are out there.

Portfolio strategy

Investors who maintain a high equity exposure probably are not my readers for the most part except for my occasional coverage of stock ETFs. For the rest of us, myself included, who set their stock/bond allocation based on their age and/or net worth (article link), making wise choices for those income assets would occupy most of our due diligence activities.

When I started investing decades ago, this process was much simpler. We had no risk CDs with high yields to use. Individual AAA- or AA-rated corporate or municipal bonds could be used to flush out that allocation. While bonds had some risks (default and interest-rate mostly), they met their purpose: income generation and portfolio volatility reduction at the possible “cost” of providing lower returns than the stock portion of the portfolio. There were preferred stocks at the time but if my memory serves me well, not high on the radar unless one's portfolio was being managed by a professional.

With the addition of low-cost ETFs to the longer existing MFs and CEFs, we now have better options and can avoid the need to build a diversified income portfolio one issue at a time and thus saving the effort of managing maybe 1-2 dozen holdings as one might be doing for their stock allocation. Funds also allow exposure to lower rated issues bundled together so individual defaults do not destroy a portfolio. All this should allow an investor to concentrate their due diligence effort to picking the best funds, sprinkling in select individual holdings like those reviewed here to achieve a successful fixed income allocation (though not these ones).
